title: Exxon Mobil’s Guyana Oil Growth Puts Governance And ESG Risks In Focus - simplywall.st
content:
For investors watching NYSE:XOM, Guyana has become a central story for future production and risk. The stock trades around $152.51, with a 1 year return of 52.9% and a 5 year return of 226.0%, underscoring how closely investors have been tracking Exxon's large projects. Those gains sit alongside a 7 day return of a 5.1% decline and a 30 day return of a 2.3% decline, which may reflect shifting sentiment as geopolitical and ESG questions move to the forefront. What now matters for shareholders is less about headline barrels and more about how Exxon manages the trade off between Guyana's growth, regional tensions, and pressure from governance focused investors. Upcoming decisions on project pace, community impact, and shareholder voting practices could influence how durable the recent enthusiasm around NYSE:XOM proves to be in portfolios. Stay updated on the most important news stories for Exxon Mobil by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Exxon Mobil. See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Exxon Mobil following this latest news. For investors, the Guyana story is now as much about governance as it is about geology. On 8 April 2026, two shareholder proposals put Exxon Mobil’s power structure and retail voting program under the microscope, just as the company leans more heavily on Guyana to offset conflict related production hits in Qatar and the UAE. One proposal pushes for an independent chair, splitting Darren Woods’ CEO and chair roles, while another questions whether Exxon's retail voting tools tilt outcomes toward the board’s own recommendations. Management’s decision to urge votes against both signals a clear preference to preserve the current model. Some investors may view this as efficient in a high risk operating environment, while others may see it as a concentration of authority at a time when ESG and geopolitical scrutiny are rising. Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story.
 Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Exxon Mobil to help decide what it's worth to you. From here, keep an eye on the 27 May 2026 annual meeting outcomes for the independent chair and retail voting proposals, and on any further engagement between Exxon, large pension funds and ESG focused investors. For Guyana, watch for updates on project timelines, any changes in territorial tensions with Venezuela and Suriname, and how Exxon's disclosures address environmental and community concerns. It is also worth tracking whether governance debates begin to influence analyst commentary on Exxon's risk profile relative to peers or show up in changes to ownership patterns by long term institutions. title: Exxon Mobil (XOM) Stock Drops 10% — But Wall Street Isn’t Walking Away - MEXC
content: Exxon Mobil opened Friday at $152.43, down about 1.6% on the day. The stock has pulled back roughly 10% so far in April after a strong 41% gain in Q1 2026. The Q1 run was fueled in part by geopolitical tension in the Persian Gulf. When reports emerged of a ceasefire and easing tensions around the Strait of Hormuz, oil prices dropped roughly 16%. That wiped out a chunk of XOM’s risk premium fast. There was also a company-specific knock. Exxon disclosed that its liquefied natural gas facilities in Qatar suffered damage from Iranian attacks. The company said the conflict could reduce overall oil-equivalent production by around 6% in Q1. Q1 earnings are still expected to come in above Q4 2025 levels. Despite the April dip, Wall Street has not turned cold on the stock. The average analyst price target sits at $159.20, with a Moderate Buy consensus. Jefferies raised its target to $184, Wells Fargo to $185, and JPMorgan to $170. Wolfe Research moved in the other direction, cutting its target to $153. Greenberg Financial Group initiated a new position in Q4, buying 11,822 units worth roughly $1.4 million. Institutional investors hold about 61.8% of the stock overall. On the insider side, VP Darrin L. Talley sold 1,080 units at $155.50 in mid-March. Insiders sold a combined 11,460 units over the past 90 days and hold just 0.03% of the stock. Exxon’s Stabroek Block — about 120 miles off the coast of Guyana — is shaping up to be one of the biggest production growth stories in the company’s history. Estimated reserves stand at roughly 11 billion oil-equivalent barrels. Production from the region was approaching 875,000 barrels per day at the end of 2025. Exxon expects output to exceed 1 million barrels per day before the end of 2026. A new project in the block called Hammerhead is set to come online later this year. Two thirds of Exxon’s total global oil-equivalent production now comes from three areas: the Permian Basin, Stabroek, and LNG operations in the Middle East. That concentration in the Western Hemisphere — largely outside Middle East chokepoints — has become a selling point for analysts. Exxon chairman Darren Woods made clear at a January White House meeting that Venezuela was “not investable,” despite pressure from the Trump administration. Guyana, just 700 miles away, tells the other side of that story. Stabroek de-risked the need for Venezuelan barrels entirely. UBS flagged a lesser-known Exxon asset in its recent coverage: the LaBarge facility in Wyoming, which produces roughly 20% of global helium supply. Qatar supplies about 31% of the global helium market. With shipping around the Strait of Hormuz disrupted, Exxon’s U.S.-based helium output offers a more stable alternative. That positions LaBarge as a potential pricing power play if Qatar supply remains constrained. Exxon’s Q1 2026 earnings report has not yet been released, and will be watched for the full impact of the Qatar LNG disruption and any production update from Stabroek. title: Oil Surges 7% on Hormuz Blockade, U.S. Gulf Tanker Rush — 3 Stocks to Buy Now - 24/7 Wall St.
content: ConocoPhillips (COP) operates low-cost Permian and Gulf of America assets generating $13.86B in Q4 2025 revenue with 2.33-2.36M barrels per day production guidance. Chevron (CVX) reported Q4 adjusted earnings of $1.52 per share with 3.8-3.9M barrels per day expected for Q1, backed by 39 consecutive years of dividend growth. Exxon Mobil (XOM) produced 4.7M oil-equivalent barrels per day in 2025 with 1.6M from the Permian alone, generating $28.8B in full-year 2025 earnings and 43 consecutive years of dividend increases. Trump’s blockade of the Strait of Hormuz sent Brent crude to $96 per barrel and WTI to $97, while rerouted tankers are now sourcing crude from U.S. Gulf Coast ports, giving ConocoPhillips, Chevron, and ExxonMobil higher realized prices and stronger export demand for their domestic production. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks. Get them here FREE. Oil prices are jumping 7% as President Trump orders a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, tightening global supply. At the same time, empty tankers originally bound for the Middle East have rerouted to U.S. Gulf Coast ports, snapping up domestic crude and giving American producers an extra lift. Brent crude jumped to $96 per barrel, according to Hyperliquid futures, while West Texas Intermediate (WTI) surged to $97. Trump said on Truth Social that the U.S. will eventually open the Strait to all ships, though he also vowed to stop any ship in international waters that previously paid the “extortion” toll Iran charged for safe passage. If Iran fires on any ship, it will be “BLOWN TO HELL!” This twin development means higher realized prices plus stronger U.S. export demand. For retail investors, ConocoPhillips (NYSE:COP | COP Price Prediction), Chevron (NYSE:CVX), and  Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) stand out because they combine low-cost domestic production with direct ties to Gulf infrastructure. Let’s walk through them. ConocoPhillips operates as a focused upstream producer with minimal refining drag, so every dollar of higher oil prices flows straight to the bottom line. Its 2026 production guidance calls for 2.33 million to 2.36 million barrels of oil equivalent per day. Much of that comes from low-cost Permian and Gulf of America assets that feed directly into the export terminals now seeing tanker traffic. Here’s what the numbers tell us. ConocoPhillips generated $13.86 billion in revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025, with adjusted earnings of $1.02 per share. Its trailing price-to-earnings ratio sits at 19.30. The forward annual dividend yield lands at 2.74%, backed by a quarterly payout of $0.84 per share. Peer comparison shows ConocoPhillips offers higher oil-price sensitivity than integrated majors while still returning capital at a 45% cash-flow payout ratio. Simply put, the Gulf rerouting tightens domestic realizations, and ConocoPhillips’ U.S.-heavy footprint positions it to capture that premium first. Chevron brings integrated scale plus heavy Gulf of America deepwater and Permian exposure. The company expects Q1 net oil-equivalent production of 3.8 million to 3.9 million barrels per day, even after Middle East downtime. That U.S. output heads straight to the same Gulf terminals welcoming rerouted tankers. Numbers back the edge. Chevron reported adjusted earnings per share of $1.52 for Q4, beating estimates by 5.56%. The forward annual dividend reaches $7.12 per share for a 3.39% yield, outpacing ConocoPhillips. Trailing P/E stands at 29.3, higher than peers, but the company’s 3.8% dividend growth streak (39 consecutive years) and Gulf infrastructure ownership deliver steadier cash flows. READ: The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks When oil rises and U.S. exports spike, Chevron’s downstream balance cushions volatility while its upstream assets capture the price pop. Granted, Middle East exposure trimmed output by roughly 6% in the quarter, yet the Gulf rerouting more than offsets that for domestic-focused barrels. Exxon Mobil delivers the largest production footprint of the three, with 4.7 million oil-equivalent barrels per day in 2025 — its highest annual total in more than 40 years. Permian output alone hit 1.6 million barrels per day, and the company targets 1.8 million in 2026. Those volumes flow through Gulf Coast terminals positioned to load tankers now detouring from Hormuz. The data shows Exxon’s enduring strength. Full-year 2025 earnings reached $28.8 billion with $52 billion in cash flow from operations and $26.1 billion in free cash flow. Shareholder distributions totaled $37.2 billion, split between $17.2 billion in dividends and $20 billion in repurchases. The quarterly dividend rose 4% to $1.03 per share for Q1, yielding 2.70% on a trailing basis with 43 consecutive years of increases. Its trailing P/E of 22.76 sits between ConocoPhillips’ lower multiple and Chevron’s higher one, reflecting scale advantages. In short, Exxon Mobil’s advantaged Permian and Gulf assets turn the blockade’s price surge and the tanker rerouting into higher volumes and stronger margins at the same time. When all is said and done, these three stocks — ConocoPhillips, Chevron, and Exxon Mobil — give everyday investors a balanced way to play both the global oil-price lift from the Hormuz blockade and the U.S. Gulf export boom. They trade at reasonable valuations relative to their cash-flow generation, pay reliable dividends backed by strong balance sheets, and hold the exact U.S. production and infrastructure that benefit most from current events. That said, energy stocks remain volatile; a quick ceasefire, the Strait reopening, or a demand slowdown could reverse gains. Consider starting small and holding for the long term. The numbers show these names have the assets and the track record to reward patient shareholders in the months ahead. title: Exxon Mobil (XOM) Stock Slides 10% in April — Analysts Still Bullish with $185 Price Targets - Blockonomi
content: Exxon Mobil began Friday trading at $152.43, representing approximately a 1.6% decline for the session. The energy giant has experienced a nearly 10% pullback throughout April after posting an impressive 41% advance during the first quarter of 2026. The first-quarter rally was partially driven by escalating geopolitical concerns in the Persian Gulf region. However, when ceasefire announcements emerged and Strait of Hormuz tensions began cooling, crude oil valuations plummeted approximately 16%. This development rapidly erased a significant portion of XOM’s geopolitical risk premium. Adding to the pressure was a company-specific challenge. Exxon revealed that Iranian military operations damaged its Qatar-based liquefied natural gas infrastructure. The corporation indicated this conflict might decrease total oil-equivalent output by approximately 6% during Q1. Nevertheless, first-quarter earnings are anticipated to exceed fourth-quarter 2025 performance. Despite April’s downturn, Wall Street sentiment toward the stock remains constructive. Analyst consensus sits at a Moderate Buy rating, with average price projections at $159.20. Jefferies elevated its target to $184, Wells Fargo pushed to $185, and JPMorgan raised to $170. Conversely, Wolfe Research reduced its target to $153. Greenberg Financial Group established a fresh stake during Q4, acquiring 11,822 shares valued at approximately $1.4 million. Institutional ownership comprises roughly 61.8% of outstanding shares. On the executive front, VP Darrin L. Talley divested 1,080 shares at $155.50 during mid-March. Company insiders collectively sold 11,460 units over the previous 90 days and maintain just 0.03% ownership. Exxon’s Stabroek Block — positioned approximately 120 miles offshore from Guyana — represents one of the corporation’s most significant production expansion opportunities in recent history. Reserve estimates total roughly 11 billion oil-equivalent barrels. Regional output was nearing 875,000 barrels daily by the conclusion of 2025. Exxon anticipates production will surpass 1 million barrels per day prior to 2026’s end. A new development within the block, designated Hammerhead, is scheduled to commence operations later this year. Two-thirds of Exxon’s global oil-equivalent production currently originates from three primary sources: the Permian Basin, Stabroek, and Middle Eastern LNG operations. This geographic concentration in the Western Hemisphere — predominantly outside Middle East vulnerability zones — has emerged as a compelling narrative for analysts. Exxon chairman Darren Woods clearly stated during a January White House discussion that Venezuela remained “not investable,” despite Trump administration encouragement. Guyana, located just 700 miles distant, illustrates the contrasting opportunity. Stabroek completely eliminated dependence on Venezuelan production. UBS highlighted an underappreciated Exxon asset in recent analysis: the Wyoming-based LaBarge facility, which generates approximately 20% of global helium supply. Qatar contributes roughly 31% of worldwide helium production. With Strait of Hormuz shipping disruptions, Exxon’s domestically-based helium operations provide a more reliable supply source. This positions LaBarge as a potential pricing advantage if Qatari supply remains disrupted. Exxon’s Q1 2026 financial results have not yet been published, and will be scrutinized for complete details regarding Qatar LNG disruption impacts and any production updates from Stabroek. title: Westbourne Investments Inc. Has $1.40 Million Stake in Exxon Mobil Corporation $XOM - MarketBeat
content:
Westbourne Investments Inc. increased its position in  Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM - Free Report) by 250.6% in the fourth quarter, according to its most recent 13F filing with the SEC. The firm owned 11,657 shares of the oil and gas company's stock after purchasing an additional 8,332 shares during the period. Westbourne Investments Inc.'s holdings in Exxon Mobil were worth $1,403,000 at the end of the most recent reporting period. A number of other institutional investors have also recently bought and sold shares of the business. He has a BA in Business from Metro State University and a Public Leadership Credential from Harvard’s Kennedy School of Government. He was the original host of Talking Drum, the signature current affairs show on the African Broadcasting Network (ABN-America), which was available nationwide in the United States via the Dish Network satellite service. title: US-Iran Ceasefire Impact on Oil Prices & Stock Market 2026 - Intellectia AI
content: The two-week ceasefire agreement between the United States and Iran has triggered unprecedented volatility in global oil markets, creating both risks and opportunities for investors. While the initial announcement sent oil prices tumbling as fears of supply disruption eased, persistent concerns about the agreement's fragility have driven Brent crude back above $99 per barrel within 24 hours. This whipsaw price action reflects the market's deep uncertainty about whether diplomatic progress can hold amid decades of hostility. For investors, the current environment demands a sophisticated approach that balances the potential for significant returns in the energy sector against geopolitical risks that could resurface without warning. Major oil companies like Exxon Mobil and Chevron have demonstrated remarkable resilience, with XOM shares gaining 41.95% year-to-date through March 31 and CVX climbing 37.09% during the same period. However, Goldman Sachs analysts warn that Brent crude could average above $100 per barrel throughout 2026 if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for another month, creating a complex risk-reward scenario that requires careful position sizing and active monitoring. The broader implications extend beyond commodity prices to encompass inflation expectations, central bank policy decisions, and the performance of energy-dependent sectors across the global economy. Understanding these interconnected dynamics is essential for navigating the weeks ahead as negotiations between the US and Iran continue to unfold. When President Donald Trump announced the two-week ceasefire agreement on April 7, 2026, markets responded with a mixture of relief and skepticism. Oil prices initially plummeted as traders priced in reduced geopolitical risk, with Brent crude falling sharply from elevated levels that had reflected fears of prolonged supply disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz. However, the relief proved short-lived as reports emerged suggesting Iran had declared the ceasefire broken, sending tremors through energy markets and reminding participants that diplomatic breakthroughs in this region are often tenuous. The price action that followed illustrated the market's fragile confidence. After the initial drop, oil prices reversed course and climbed back above $99 per barrel within 24 hours as concerns about the ceasefire's durability intensified. This volatility underscores a fundamental reality: even with diplomatic progress, the structural risks to global oil supplies through the Strait of Hormuz remain elevated. The strait handles approximately 20% of global petroleum consumption, making any threat to its openness a matter of immediate concern for energy markets worldwide. Goldman Sachs has attempted to quantify these risks with revised forecasts that reflect the uncertainty. The investment bank trimmed its second-quarter 2026 Brent crude forecast to $90 per barrel from $99, and its WTI forecast to $87 per barrel from $91, citing reduced geopolitical risk premium following the ceasefire announcement. However, these projections come with a significant caveat: Goldman warns that Brent could average above $100 per barrel throughout 2026 if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed for another month. This dual-scenario forecasting highlights the binary nature of the current investment environment. If negotiations succeed and shipping lanes reopen fully, oil prices could normalize at lower levels, compressing margins for producers but providing relief to energy-dependent industries and consumers. Conversely, a breakdown in talks and continued Hormuz closure would likely sustain elevated prices, benefiting oil producers while potentially triggering broader economic consequences through higher inflation and reduced consumer spending power. The energy sector has been the standout performer in the S&P 500 during the first quarter of 2026, with the XLE Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund gaining over 34% as oil prices surged on geopolitical concerns. This performance has been driven primarily by the supermajors—integrated oil giants with the scale and diversification to capitalize on elevated commodity prices while weathering volatility better than their smaller competitors. Exxon Mobil has emerged as a particular standout, with analysts at Citi and TD Cowen recently raising price targets to $175 from previous levels of $150 and $145 respectively. The company has successfully lowered its breakeven point to approximately $40-42 per barrel through strategic high-grading of assets in Guyana and the Permian Basin. This operational efficiency means that even if Brent crude averages $60 in 2026—a scenario that would squeeze margins for less efficient producers—Exxon would remain solidly profitable while generating substantial free cash flow for shareholder returns. Chevron has similarly demonstrated strength, with Truist analysts upgrading the energy sector to "Attractive" from "Neutral" earlier this week as oil prices climbed back toward $100 per barrel. The company's integrated business model, combining upstream production with downstream refining and marketing operations, provides natural hedging against price volatility while capturing value across the entire petroleum value chain. The recent 4% pullback in both Exxon and Chevron shares, observed in early April, represents what analysts describe as a "corrective mechanism" that stripped away speculative froth accumulated during the first quarter rally. This consolidation leaves behind a more grounded assessment of the sector's health and potentially creates entry points for investors who missed the initial surge but remain bullish on energy's medium-term prospects. Looking ahead to first-quarter earnings, analysts anticipate solid results from the majors, with expectations for Exxon of approximately $1.80 EPS, representing a 2.3% increase from the previous year. These results will provide crucial insights into how efficiently these companies have managed costs and capital deployment during the volatile pricing environment of early 2026. The trajectory of oil prices over the coming months carries significant implications for the broader global economy, particularly regarding inflation dynamics and central bank policy responses. When the Commerce Department revised down the annual rate of economic growth in the fourth quarter of 2025 to 0.5% from a previously reported 0.7%, it highlighted the economy's vulnerability to external shocks—including sustained elevated energy costs. Australia's central bank has already flagged these concerns, warning that the Iran conflict has raised inflation worries while also creating risks to economic growth. This stagflationary scenario—where inflation rises while growth stagnates—represents a worst-case outcome for policymakers, who typically face difficult trade-offs between supporting economic activity and controlling price pressures. For consumers, the impact has been immediate and tangible. Gasoline and diesel prices have continued climbing despite the ceasefire announcement, reflecting the lag between crude oil movements and retail fuel costs as well as persistent concerns about supply security. These higher energy costs ripple through the economy, affecting everything from transportation and logistics to manufacturing and agriculture, potentially forcing the Federal Reserve to maintain higher interest rates for longer than previously anticipated. The inflationary implications extend beyond direct energy costs to encompass broader inflation expectations. When consumers and businesses see persistently high oil prices, they tend to factor continued inflation into their decision-making, potentially creating self-fulfilling prophecies as workers demand higher wages and businesses raise prices in anticipation of cost increases. Navigating the current energy market requires a multi-faceted approach that accounts for both upside potential and downside risks. For investors considering energy sector exposure, several strategies warrant consideration: Quality Focus: The supermajors—Exxon Mobil, Chevron, and their international peers—offer the most attractive risk-adjusted returns in the current environment. Their scale, diversification, and financial strength provide insulation against volatility that smaller producers cannot match. The valuation metrics for these companies remain reasonable relative to their cash flow generation capabilities, particularly if oil prices remain elevated. Dividend Security: Energy majors have historically offered attractive dividend yields, and the current cash flow environment supports continued or enhanced shareholder payouts. For income-focused investors, the combination of yield potential and capital appreciation makes these stocks attractive relative to fixed-income alternatives in a higher-rate environment. Options Strategies: For investors seeking to capitalize on volatility rather than simply endure it, options strategies such as covered calls or cash-secured puts can generate additional income while providing defined-risk exposure to the sector. The elevated implied volatility in energy options creates favorable premium collection opportunities for sophisticated investors. Diversification Considerations: While direct energy exposure offers significant upside, investors should ensure their portfolios can withstand potential downside scenarios. A sustained diplomatic breakthrough that normalizes oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz could trigger significant price declines, making position sizing and stop-loss discipline essential risk management tools. While short-term trading opportunities dominate current market discussions, the broader energy transition continues to reshape the sector's long-term investment landscape. The current crisis highlights the ongoing importance of petroleum to the global economy—even as renewable energy capacity expands, the transportation and industrial sectors remain heavily dependent on fossil fuels, creating inelastic demand that amplifies price volatility when supply disruptions occur. For investors with multi-year time horizons, the current environment may represent an opportunity to acquire quality energy assets at reasonable valuations before the sector potentially benefits from both continued hydrocarbon demand and strategic positioning for the energy transition. Companies that can generate substantial cash flows from traditional operations while investing selectively in lower-carbon technologies offer a balanced approach to capturing value across the energy spectrum. The impact of oil price volatility extends beyond commodity and equity markets to encompass currency and regional equity dynamics. Oil-exporting nations generally benefit from elevated prices, with their currencies and equity markets outperforming during periods of commodity strength. Conversely, oil-importing emerging markets face headwinds from higher energy costs and potential capital outflows as investors seek safer havens. Asia-Pacific markets have demonstrated mixed responses to the evolving situation. While Japan's Nikkei and South Korea's Kospi have faced pressure from energy import costs and global growth concerns, Australia's resource-heavy market has benefited from commodity strength. These divergent performances highlight the importance of geographic diversification in managing portfolio risk during commodity-driven market cycles. The US-Iran ceasefire represents a pivotal moment for energy markets, offering the potential for reduced geopolitical risk while simultaneously highlighting the fragility of diplomatic progress in one of the world's most volatile regions. For investors, the key takeaway is that volatility is likely to persist regardless of whether negotiations ultimately succeed or fail. The energy sector's strong performance in early 2026 reflects genuine fundamental improvements in the operating environment for producers, but also embeds significant geopolitical risk premiums that could unwind quickly if diplomatic solutions prove durable. Investors should approach the sector with clear-eyed realism about both the opportunities and risks, focusing on quality companies with strong balance sheets and efficient operations that can thrive across various price scenarios. As negotiations between the US and Iran continue, maintaining appropriate position sizes, utilizing diversification strategies, and staying informed about developments will be essential for navigating what promises to be a volatile but potentially rewarding investment environment. The energy sector's critical role in the global economy ensures that it will remain a focal point for investors seeking both income and growth in an uncertain world. title: Exxon Mobil (XOM.US) is expected to report a profit increase of up to $2.9 billion in its upstream business for the first quarter. - 富途牛牛
content: Exxon Mobil (XOM.US) stated on Wednesday that due to the surge in oil prices driven by the Iran conflict, the company’s upstream business profits for the first quarter will increase by up to $2.9 billion, offsetting the impact of partial disruptions in its oil and gas production in the Middle East. The company added that its oil and natural gas production in the first quarter will decline by 6% compared to the fourth quarter, while downstream business profits may be impacted by approximately $5.3 billion, partly due to timing effects. However, Exxon Mobil noted that its profits will improve later as oil and gas deliveries are completed. The translation is provided by third-party software. title: This is Why Chevron Stock Remains a Buy After Its Q1 Outlook - The Globe and Mail
content: Chevron CorporationCVX is emerging as one of the biggest winners from the recent surge in oil prices caused by the conflict in the Middle East. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz and disruptions to oil and gas production across the region have pushed crude prices sharply higher, creating a favorable backdrop for large energy companies. While many oil majors are dealing with major operational setbacks, Chevron appears better positioned because of its relatively low exposure to the Middle East. That combination of rising commodity prices, resilient operations and improving earnings expectations makes CVX an attractive stock for investors looking for energy exposure in 2026. Chevron recently said that its first-quarter 2026 upstream earnings could rise by $1.6 billion to $2.2 billion from the prior quarter because of stronger oil and natural gas prices. Brent crude averaged close to $80 per barrel during the period, rising sharply from the previous quarter, following supply disruptions linked to the Iran conflict.The key issue has been the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for global energy flows. Supply concerns have pushed commodity prices higher, benefiting companies with large upstream exposure. Chevron is expected to be among the major beneficiaries, as only a very small portion of its liquids production comes from the Middle East.This gives Chevron an edge over peers like ExxonMobilXOM and Shell plcSHEL. ExxonMobil has a modest share of production linked to the region, while European majors such as Shell have a more meaningful exposure. As a result, Chevron can benefit from higher oil prices with relatively lower operational risk compared to ExxonMobil and Shell. Chevron still expects first-quarter production to fall to 3.8-3.9 million barrels of oil equivalent per day (MMBOE/d) from just more than 4 MMBOE/d in the fourth quarter of 2025. The decline is mainly tied to downtime at the Tengizchevroil project in Kazakhstan and lower output from assets in Israel and the Saudi-Kuwaiti Partitioned Zone.However, these disruptions appear temporary rather than structural. Chevron continues to target production growth of 7% to 10% in 2026, excluding asset sales. The company is ramping up several major projects, including Guyana, the Gulf of America, the Permian Basin and the Eastern Mediterranean.Chevron also continues to improve efficiency across its business. Management has already achieved $1.5 billion in structural cost reductions and is targeting $3 billion to $4 billion in savings by the end of 2026. These efforts are improving margins and strengthening free cash flow generation. Meanwhile, ExxonMobil and Shell are facing more severe production challenges tied directly to the Middle East conflict. ExxonMobil expects a 6% production decline because of disruptions in Qatar and the UAE, while Shell is dealing with lower gas output and damage to infrastructure in Qatar. Compared with ExxonMobil and Shell, Chevron’s production base appears more resilient. Chevron stock has already rewarded investors, rising more than 40% over the past year. That easily beats the S&P 500, which has delivered a gain in the low-30% range during the same period. Analysts are also becoming more optimistic about the company’s earnings outlook. Over the past 30 days, the Zacks Consensus Estimate for Chevron’s 2026 and 2027 earnings per share has moved higher, mirroring estimate increases for ExxonMobil and Shell. Chevron also remains attractive from a cash return perspective. The company has increased its dividend for 38 consecutive years and currently offers a yield of 3.7%. It repurchased roughly $12 billion of stock over the past year, and stronger oil prices could support even larger buybacks in 2026.From a valuation standpoint, Chevron trades at a premium to ExxonMobil and Shell based on forward price-to-earnings ratios. While that premium may seem expensive at first glance, investors appear willing to pay more for Chevron because of its lower Middle East exposure, a stronger balance sheet, production growth outlook and ability to generate cash even during volatile markets. Chevron stands out as one of the best-positioned major oil companies heading into the rest of 2026. Higher oil prices are creating a major earnings tailwind, while the company’s limited exposure to the Middle East reduces the risk of deeper production disruptions. Chevron also has multiple growth projects, strong free cash flow, rising earnings estimates and a long history of rewarding shareholders through dividends and buybacks. Although valuation is somewhat higher than ExxonMobil and Shell, Chevron deserves that premium because of its stronger risk-reward profile. With improving fundamentals and supportive energy prices, Chevron stock is currently a Zacks Rank #1 (Strong Buy).You can seethe complete list of today’s Zacks #1 Rank stocks here. And it's just beginning to enter the spotlight, which is exactly where you want to be. With strong earnings growth and an expanding customer base, it's positioned to feed the rampant demand for Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, and Internet of Things. title: Earnings Preview: What To Expect From Exxon Mobil's Report - FinancialContent
content: Spring, Texas-based Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) engages in the exploration and production of crude oil and natural gas in the United States and internationally. Valued at a market cap of $646 billion, the company operates through Upstream, Energy Products, Chemical Products, and Specialty Products segments. XOM is expected to release its Q1 2026 earnings soon. Ahead of the event, analysts expect the company’s EPS to be $1.80 on a diluted basis, up 2.3% from $1.76 in the year-ago quarter. The company has exceeded Wall Street’s EPS estimates in all of its last four quarters. For fiscal 2026, analysts project the company’s EPS to be $8.73, up 24.9% from $6.99 in fiscal 2025. Moreover, its EPS is expected to rise by roughly 5.5% year over year (YoY) to $9.21 in fiscal 2027. XOM stock has surged 54.7% over the past 52 weeks, outperforming the S&P 500 Index’s ($SPX) 25.1% rise, and the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF’s (XLE) 39.2% return during the same time frame. XOM has been capturing the attention of investors interested in energy stocks for many years now, and it is no different now, even amid volatility in the Middle East. As President Trump announces a two-week ceasefire and a 10-point proposal for Iran, oil prices have been in an uncertain state. However, XOM stands tall amidst this, offering strong financials and fundamentals. The company boasts diversified operations, including upstream production, refining, chemicals, and LNG, which are set to generate revenue throughout different price cycles. Moreover, the company announced 4.7 million oil-equivalent barrels per day worth of production in its Q4 2025 earnings, the highest in more than 40 years, indicating operational excellence. Analysts are moderately bullish about XOM, with the stock having a “Moderate Buy” rating overall. Among the 28 analysts covering the stock, 14 are recommending a “Strong Buy,” one recommends “Moderate Buy,” 11 recommend “Hold,” and two suggest a “Strong Sell” for the stock. XOM’s average analyst price target is $157.96, indicating an upside of 1.9% from the current levels. We won't sell or share your personal information to inform the ads you see. What gives ExxonMobil its competitive advantage in energy? One of the successor companies to John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil monopoly that was broken up in 1911, ExxonMobil (NYSE:XOM) explores for and produces crude oil and natural gas, refines and sells petroleum products, and manufactures petrochemicals. In Energy, scale separates fragile single-asset producers from platform-style businesses that generate revenue across entire basins and infrastructure networks. ExxonMobil’s $332.2 billion of revenue in the last year is top-tier for the industry, suggesting the company has hit a level of diversification where investors can sleep easy at night. Free cash flow isn't a prominently featured metric in company financials and earnings releases, but we think it's telling because it accounts for all operating and capital expenses, making it tough to manipulate. Cash is king. ExxonMobil has shown impressive cash profitability, giving it the option to reinvest or return capital to investors. The company’s free cash flow margin averaged 10.6% over the last five years, better than the broader energy upstream and integrated energy sector. In any given year, energy gross margins are heavily influenced by prices, hedging, and cost inflation, but over a full cycle these gross margins reveal which producers are structurally advantaged through superior “rock” quality, infrastructure access, and cost position. ExxonMobil, which averaged 44.3% gross margin over the last five years, exhibits subpar unit economics in the sector. It means the company will struggle more at lower commodity prices than peers with better gross margins. title: Exxon Mobil (XOM) Stock Reverses As Oil Rally Loses Steam - Sahm
content:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) Stock Reverses As Oil Rally Loses Steam Exxon Mobil Corp (NYSE:XOM) shares were initially trading higher Thursday morning as oil prices jumped on renewed Middle East tension, but the stock gave back those gains and turned lower by the afternoon as crude sharply trimmed its advance and broader market sentiment rotated away from the worst-case energy-shock scenario. The early setup looked supportive for Exxon because the news backdrop still carried real supply-risk implications. President Donald Trump said U.S. military assets would remain positioned near Iran until a real agreement is fully complied with, while Tehran accused Washington of breaching ceasefire terms and reiterated threats against vessels waiting to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, which remained shut. For Exxon, that matters directly because it is one of the world's largest integrated oil companies, with earnings power highly sensitive to crude prices across its upstream production business. But Thursday’s reversal makes sense because the afternoon tape started pricing in less immediate escalation. Israel agreed to engage after Lebanon requested direct talks, introducing a fragile de-escalation angle that helped stocks rebound more broadly while oil pared gains after briefly moving back above $100 a barrel. By midday, WTI was up 1.4% at $95.75, meaning crude had already come off its highs. That kind of oil fade can pressure XOM intraday because traders often buy Exxon on tightening supply fears and then sell when crude loses momentum. XOM's RSI has largely remained in the neutral range between 40 and 65 over the past year, indicating steady but not extreme momentum. There are several brief spikes above 70, especially more recently, suggesting short-term overbought conditions that tend to fade quickly rather than sustain extended rallies. Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $151.76. Recent analyst moves include: XOM Price Action: Exxon Mobil shares were down 0.36% at $155.68 at the time of publication on Thursday, according to Benzinga Pro data. title: Exxon Mobil (XOM) Stock: Why Analysts Still Like It After a Big Run-Up - MEXC
content: Exxon Mobil’s (XOM) bull case remains intact despite a wave of conflicting signals from Wall Street and the Middle East. The company is navigating geopolitical disruption while analysts continue to raise their targets. Jefferies lifted its price target from $178 to $184 on Thursday, maintaining a “buy” rating. That implies roughly 18.25% upside from recent closing prices. Wells Fargo also upgraded XOM to “overweight” with a $185 target, adding fresh momentum for buyers. Not everyone is as upbeat. Wolfe Research trimmed its target from $158 to $153, and BMO Capital Markets kept a “market perform” rating with a $155 price target. Overall, the Wall Street consensus sits at “Moderate Buy,” based on 13 Buy, seven Hold, and one Sell rating, with an average target of $157.42. XOM was trading at $155.61 on Thursday, down slightly on the day. The stock carries a market cap of roughly $648 billion, a P/E ratio of 23.30, and a 50-day moving average of $154. On the earnings front, XOM beat Q4 expectations. The company posted EPS of $1.71, ahead of the $1.63 consensus. Revenue came in at $80.04 billion, topping the $77.98 billion estimate, though that was still down 1.3% year over year. The Iran conflict is creating real headwinds for Exxon in the near term. The company disclosed that roughly 6% of its global production was disrupted. Management also flagged that Q1 upstream and downstream results would take a hit. On the upside, Exxon said higher crude and gas prices from the conflict could boost Q1 upstream profits by as much as $2.9 billion. A temporary pause in attacks near the Strait of Hormuz lifted market sentiment briefly, sending crude prices lower and pressuring XOM early in the session. But shipping through the strait remains impaired, and insurers are still cautious. Exxon’s growth story outside the Middle East is a key part of the bull case. Production from the Stabroek Block in Guyana has grown quickly and now ranks as one of the company’s top growth engines. The Pioneer Natural Resources acquisition has also found its stride in the Permian Basin, giving Exxon a larger, lower-cost production base in West Texas. The Permian’s short-cycle nature means Exxon can respond faster to market shifts than rivals tied to more politically constrained projects. XOM is up around 61% over the past year, which has some analysts questioning how much upside is left. At roughly 21x 2026 estimated EPS of $7.4, it trades above the historical range for oil majors. Exxon’s 12-month high is $176.41; its 12-month low was $97.80. Insiders sold 11,460 shares worth $1.69 million in the last quarter. Institutional investors hold 61.80% of the stock. Analysts at Erste Group lifted their FY2026–FY2027 EPS forecasts for Exxon, citing improving core earnings expectations. The average analyst EPS forecast for the full year sits at $7.43. title: Exxon Mobil (XOM) Stock: Wall Street Analysts Boost Targets Despite Geopolitical Headwinds - Blockonomi
content: Exxon Mobil’s (XOM) investment thesis continues to hold strength even as the company faces mixed Wall Street perspectives and geopolitical challenges. The energy giant is managing international disruptions while several analysts elevate their price projections. On Thursday, Jefferies increased its price objective from $178 to $184, holding firm on its “buy” recommendation. This new target suggests approximately 18.25% potential appreciation from current trading levels. Wells Fargo also joined the optimistic camp, elevating XOM to “overweight” with a $185 price goal, providing additional validation for bullish investors. However, not all analysts share this enthusiasm. Wolfe Research reduced its price target from $158 down to $153, while BMO Capital Markets maintained a “market perform” stance with a $155 objective. The aggregate Wall Street view reflects a “Moderate Buy” rating, derived from 13 Buy recommendations, seven Hold ratings, and one Sell call, yielding a mean price target of $157.42. XOM closed Thursday at $155.61, experiencing a modest decline during the trading session. The energy company commands a market capitalization near $648 billion, trades at a P/E multiple of 23.30, and maintains a 50-day moving average of $154. Regarding quarterly performance, XOM exceeded Wall Street’s fourth-quarter projections. The company delivered earnings per share of $1.71, outperforming the consensus estimate of $1.63. Total revenue registered at $80.04 billion, beating the anticipated $77.98 billion figure, despite representing a 1.3% year-over-year decline. The ongoing Iran situation presents tangible near-term challenges for Exxon. Company disclosures revealed that approximately 6% of total global production experienced disruption. Leadership also indicated that first-quarter upstream and downstream segments would face headwinds. Conversely, Exxon noted that elevated crude oil and natural gas prices stemming from the conflict could enhance Q1 upstream earnings by up to $2.9 billion. A brief cessation of hostilities near the Strait of Hormuz temporarily improved market outlook, driving crude prices downward and weighing on XOM shares during early trading. Nevertheless, maritime traffic through the critical waterway remains compromised, and insurance providers continue exercising caution. Exxon’s expansion narrative beyond Middle Eastern operations forms a critical element of the bullish investment case. Output from the Stabroek Block offshore Guyana has expanded rapidly and currently represents one of the company’s premier growth platforms. The acquisition of Pioneer Natural Resources has also proven successful in the Permian Basin, providing Exxon with an expanded, lower-cost production platform in West Texas. The Permian’s quick-turnaround development cycle enables Exxon to adapt more rapidly to market dynamics compared to competitors dependent on projects in politically sensitive regions. XOM has surged approximately 61% during the past twelve months, prompting some analysts to question remaining upside potential. Trading at roughly 21 times projected 2026 EPS of $7.4, the stock sits above the historical valuation range typical for integrated oil majors. Exxon’s twelve-month peak stands at $176.41, while its low reached $97.80. Company insiders disposed of 11,460 shares valued at $1.69 million during the most recent quarter. Institutional ownership represents 61.80% of outstanding shares. Analysts at Erste Group elevated their FY2026–FY2027 earnings per share projections for Exxon, attributing the revision to strengthening core profitability outlooks. The consensus analyst EPS forecast for the complete fiscal year stands at $7.43. Discover top-performing stocks in AI, Crypto, and Technology with expert analysis.
